results.title: Axe Compute’s $260 Million GPU Deal Sends Stock Soaring More Than 80%
results.author: CryptoProwl
results.content:
Axe Compute shares (NASDAQ: $AGPU $NVDA
The size of the market reaction reflects more than just the headline value. The agreement gives Axe a much larger proof point for the business it has been trying to build around dedicated enterprise GPU capacity, at a time when buyers are increasingly looking for alternatives to shared cloud environments and hyperscaler supply limits.
The company said the deployment is intended for large-scale model training, fine-tuning and high-throughput inference, with 4.8 megawatts of dedicated power delivered on an N+1 redundant basis and a targeted start in Q3 2026.
The infrastructure story underneath the deal is also part of why it drew so much attention. Aethir said the contract is being powered by its decentralized GPU cloud, which lets Axe package enterprise-grade capacity without having to build all of the underlying data center footprint itself. That pushes the deal beyond a single customer win and closer to a larger test of whether distributed GPU supply can be turned into something contractable, reliable and enterprise-ready at scale.
Axe CEO Christopher Miglino said the agreement signals that enterprise AI customers are no longer willing to shape their infrastructure roadmaps around legacy cloud constraints. That probably captures the larger takeaway here.
The AI compute market is still being defined by hardware scarcity, but investors are starting to reward the companies that can turn that scarcity into long-dated contracted revenue with clearer delivery terms.
Axe Compute Inc. (NASDAQ: AGPU) stock is currently trading at $7.22 U.S. per share.
results.summary:
Axe Compute has secured a major agreement to provide dedicated enterprise GPU capacity for large-scale AI model training, signaling a shift toward decentralized infrastructure solutions for corporate clients.

### Key Points
- Axe Compute will deploy 4.8 megawatts of dedicated power for AI training, fine-tuning, and high-throughput inference.
- The infrastructure project is scheduled to begin operations in the third quarter of 2026.
- Aethir is providing the decentralized GPU cloud technology that enables Axe to deliver enterprise-grade capacity without building traditional data centers.
- The deployment utilizes an N+1 redundant power configuration to ensure high reliability for enterprise users.
- Axe Compute (NASDAQ: AGPU) shares are currently trading at $7.22 per share following the announcement.

### Why it Matters
This deal serves as a critical test for whether decentralized GPU networks can reliably replace traditional hyperscaler cloud environments for large-scale AI workloads. By securing long-dated contracts, companies like Axe are demonstrating a viable path to overcoming current hardware scarcity and infrastructure constraints.

results.title: I found an Android TV launcher that just works better than Google's
results.author: Sanuj Bhatia
results.content:
Of all the good things Google TV does, like acting as a smart home panel and offering hundreds of free streaming channels, one thing that has always bothered me is the default app launcher.
I've complained about this before, but the default Google TV launcher isn't up to the mark. It feels slow and not very intuitive.
Plus, it comes with several tabs on the home screen, like Discover and Shop, that not everyone really needs. On top of that, the ads sprinkled throughout the interface don't help the experience.
That pushed me to try a few alternative Android TV launchers. I recently wrote about the Projectivy TV Launcher, which fixed most of my issues, but I still felt like there was more that could be improved.
Over the past week or so, I've tried another launcher, and I think I've finally found one that's actually worth switching to.
Arc Launcher brings an Apple TV-like experience to Android TV
It feels cleaner, faster, and more focused than the default launcher
The core problem with the Google TV launcher is that it tries to do too much at once. You have recommendations, live previews, multiple tabs, and your app list all crammed into one screen, which makes it harder to navigate.
Having used Apple TV for over a year now, one thing it gets absolutely right is simplicity. It puts your apps front and center and keeps everything else out of the way. And that's exactly what the Arc TV Launcher tries to bring to Android TV.
Android TV launchers
Trivia challenge
From stock interfaces to custom setups — how well do you know the launchers powering Android TV?
What was the name of Google's original Android TV launcher interface introduced in 2014?
Which third-party launcher became one of the most popular replacements for the stock Android TV interface, known for its highly customizable grid layout?
What major content-focused feature did Google TV's launcher introduce that distinguished it from the older Android TV Leanback Launcher?
Which streaming device shipped with a heavily customized Android TV launcher called the 'Fire TV' interface, diverging significantly from Google's stock experience?
FLauncher is a popular open-source Android TV launcher. Where is its source code publicly hosted?
In what year did Google officially rebrand the Android TV interface to Google TV, starting with the Chromecast with Google TV device?
What does the 'Sideload Launcher' app primarily help users accomplish on Android TV devices?
Which of the following Android TV launcher features is specifically designed to assist users who navigate using only a D-pad remote?
Your Score
Thanks for playing!
The developer mentioned on Reddit that they built Arc Launcher after getting frustrated with the performance of the default Google TV experience. It's based on LtvLauncher and focuses primarily on speed and simplicity.
Arc Launcher clearly takes inspiration from Apple TV, and the developer isn't trying to hide that.
As soon as you install it, you're greeted with a clean (and bare bones) home screen that looks very similar to Apple TV. There's a dock at the bottom with your favorite apps, and all your other apps sit below it.
Like Apple TV, you won't find ads or content recommendations cluttering the home screen. The focus here is purely on launching apps and switching between them quickly.
The dock also has a subtle blur effect, giving it that familiar Apple TV look. You also get quick shortcuts for settings and Wi-Fi in the upper-left corner, along with the date and time in the upper-right corner.
Like any Android launcher, Arc is customizable. You can change wallpapers, tweak layouts, and adjust how everything looks.
You can use built-in gradient wallpapers or even your own photos and videos. There's also an option to change wallpapers based on the time of day, switching to darker tones at night.
Beyond that, you can create custom categories for your apps. For example, you can group apps under sections like Sports, Music, or Entertainment. You can also reorder, remove, or hide apps entirely from the main view.
I've been using Arc Launcher for over a week now, and it has already received an update that fixed a few performance issues I noticed earlier.
App launches are quick, and it feels much smoother compared to the default Google TV launcher on my device.
Google should make it easier to switch Android TV launchers
Right now, changing the default launcher is more complicated than it should be
The only major issue I've faced so far is that the app isn't available on the Google Play Store.
Arc Launcher is an open source project, and while you can download and install it from GitHub, you can't just install it directly from the Play Store. That also highlights a bigger problem with Google TV as a whole.
On Android smartphones, switching to a custom launcher is simple. You install a new one, press the home button, and select it as the default.
But on Android TV, it doesn't work that way. Google doesn't make it easy for users to switch to a different launcher on its devices. Even after installing a custom launcher, you still see the Google TV launcher when you press the home button.
Because of that, you have to rely on workarounds. I used the Button Mapper app on my Xiaomi TV to map the home button to launch Arc Launcher, but this is clearly not the ideal solution. This is something Google really needs to fix.
Arc Launcher is easily my new favorite Android TV app
Sure, some may say going for an Apple TV-like UI on Android feels a bit cheap. Still, Arc Launcher has been running flawlessly on my 5-year-old Xiaomi TV, and that's exactly the kind of experience I expect Google to deliver with its own launcher.
Until that happens, this is a solid third-party option to rely on.
On that note, if your Android TV has started to feel slow, there are a few other tricks you can try to speed it up.
results.summary:
The new open-source Arc Launcher for Android TV offers a streamlined, ad-free interface inspired by Apple TV, providing users with a faster and more customizable alternative to Google TV.

### Key Points
- Arc Launcher is an open-source project designed to improve performance and simplicity on Android TV devices.
- The interface features a dock-based layout, custom app categories, and dynamic wallpaper options without intrusive ads or content recommendations.
- The launcher is currently unavailable on the Google Play Store and must be sideloaded via GitHub.
- Users often require third-party tools like Button Mapper to set custom launchers as the default due to Google's restrictive interface settings.

### Why it Matters
This development highlights growing user frustration with the cluttered, ad-heavy nature of the default Google TV interface. It underscores a significant demand for more user-centric, lightweight software options on streaming hardware that Google currently makes difficult to customize.
